title: Score Effects: How Teams Play Ahead vs Behind
headline: When a team pulls ahead it slows down and plays it safe: a possession with a 10-plus lead runs 16.5s vs 15.6s when trailing by 10-plus (+0.9s), and a Q4 team up 15-plus scores just 105 per 100 (vs 114 up 1-9) as it eases off. The 'trailing teams chuck 3s' idea is weak here — the 3-point rate barely moves with the score.
method_note: Every possession is tagged by the ball-handling team's score margin at the moment it begins. Possessions estimated as attempts minus offensive rebounds plus turnovers plus 0.44x free-throw attempts; pace as game-clock seconds per possession. Regular season only.

findings.confirmed_slowdown: Leaders slow down. Seconds per possession climbs from 15.6s (trailing 10+) to 16.5s (leading 10+); the gradient holds in Q4 and across the multi-year pool.
findings.confirmed_foul_game: Fouling to stop the clock shows up as the leader's free-throw rate: a Q4 team up 1-9 attempts 0.48 free throws per field-goal attempt (vs ~0.30 at other states) because trailing opponents foul it deliberately.
findings.confirmed_ease_off: Big Q4 leaders ease off: leading 15+ they score 105 per 100 vs 114 when up only 1-9, and run +9 fewer points per 100 at a slower tempo (garbage-time effect).
findings.not_confirmed_three_rate: The common 'trailing teams shoot more 3s' claim is NOT borne out in aggregate: leading-10+ shot 36% threes vs 33% trailing-10+ (bench/garbage-time chucking offsets desperation heaves). Shot mix is a weak, noisy score-effect lever.

robustness.note: Direction re-checked on a wider multi-year pool; magnitudes below.

sim_recommendation.proposal: Add ONE state-dependent knob, not three. The robust, sign-stable score effect is TEMPO plus late-lead efficiency dampening: as a team's lead grows, lengthen its average possession toward the measured curve (~16.5s leading 10+ vs ~15.3-15.7s trailing) and, in the last period with a large lead, dampen its scoring rate (garbage-time factor, roughly the 105 vs 114 per-100 gap up 15+ vs up 1-9). Model the late close-game FT/foul dynamic separately (the leader's free-throw rate spikes from intentional fouls).
sim_recommendation.explicitly_do_not: Do NOT add a state-dependent 3-point-rate knob. The data does not support 'trailing teams shoot more 3s' in aggregate; a shot-mix adjustment would encode noise.
sim_recommendation.why_it_matters: A flat-tempo simulator over-samples fast, high-variance possessions for a team that is actually milking the clock with a big lead, which widens the modeled final-margin spread and can misprice close late-game and blowout tails.
sim_recommendation.caveats: Correlation, not proven cause: leaders are often the stronger team, so part of the slowdown is team quality, not pure clock strategy — a state knob must not double-count strength the ratings already carry. The extreme buckets (10+ ahead/behind and the Q4 blowout cut) carry far fewer possessions than the middle buckets. Treat this as an A/B PROPOSAL: back-test a state-adjusted tempo/dampening against the flat baseline on held-out games for margin calibration and interval coverage before any promote. No production simulator is changed here.

index_row.title: Score Effects: Playing Ahead vs Behind
index_row.headline: When a team pulls ahead it slows down and plays it safe: a possession with a 10-plus lead runs 16.5s vs 15.6s when trailing by 10-plus (+0.9s), and a Q4 team up 15-plus scores just 105 per 100 (vs 114 up 1-9) as it eases off. The 'trailing teams chuck 3s' idea is weak here — the 3-point rate barely moves with the score.
index_row.blurb: How pace, shot choice and fouling shift with the scoreboard state, and how the simulators could use it.
